This sequence ensures that the user is incentivized by the deal before they are asked to consider the long-term commitment of a subscription. The use of the asterisk before "free" creates a professional, transparent expectation of terms without cluttering the primary offer.

**Word choice.** The phrase "every order" is a critical selection, as it clarifies that the 30% discount is not a one-time trial but a persistent benefit of the subscription, increasing the perceived lifetime value. The term "autopilot" is a powerful metaphor for convenience; it positions the subscription not as a recurring bill, but as a cognitive relief—a system that manages hydration so the user doesn't have to. 

**Execution and Punctuation.** The use of the period after "sling bag" creates a hard stop that allows the offer to breathe before transitioning into the brand promise. "Hydration on autopilot starts here" acts as a definitive call to action that signals the beginning of a journey. The brevity of the copy—just 13 words—ensures high readability and impact, particularly for mobile users or those scanning quickly through a feed. The tone is authoritative yet frictionless, removing the "work" from the buying process.

hey — can you help me write a really short ad for signed copies of my horror book? it’s about undead stuff / the undead, and there are only a limited number of signed copies available.

yep — i’d keep it super spare and let the premise do the work:

"Since the dawn of time people across the globe have feared The Undead." Get a signed copy before they run out.

the move here is that it opens like a line pulled straight out of the book instead of sounding like an ad. the quotation marks matter a lot: they make the first sentence feel like a dramatic excerpt, a trailer line, or the opening voiceover to something bigger. that gives the copy instant atmosphere before it ever starts selling.

“Since the dawn of time” goes huge right away. it gives the subject mythic scale, not just “this is a horror book” but “this fear is ancient.” then “people across the globe” widens it even more, so the undead feel like a universal human fear rather than a niche monster. the line is broad, almost ceremonial, which makes the eventual sales ask feel sharper by contrast.

the lack of a comma after “time” is doing something too. grammatically, you might expect a pause, but skipping it makes the sentence roll forward like an old legend being recited. it feels less polished-ad-copy and more like a stark proclamation. that slightly breathless rhythm helps the line feel ominous instead of tidy.

capitalizing “The Undead” is another strong choice. it turns the phrase into a proper force, almost a title or category of ancient enemy. “the undead” would just name a creature type; “The Undead” makes them feel like a known, feared presence. that capitalization gives the monster side of the ad weight without adding extra explanation.

then the second sentence snaps out of the mythic register: “Get a signed copy before they run out.” that shift is why the ad works. it goes from timeless dread to a very concrete, immediate action. no long description, no plot summary, no genre explanation. just: this thing exists, it’s signed, and it may not be available for long.

“signed copy” is the value driver, placed right after the atmospheric quote so the product feels collectible rather than generic. and “before they run out” creates urgency without sounding overbuilt. it’s plain language, almost casual, which keeps the ad from becoming melodramatic. the first sentence carries the drama; the second sentence closes the sale.

the whole ad is only two sentences, but the sequence is clean: atmosphere first, scarcity second. the first sentence makes someone curious; the second gives them a reason to act now. that’s why the brevity helps — horror readers don’t need a full pitch if the vibe is clear and the object is limited.

Need ad for KnightsbridgeFX — Canadian currency exchange service. Facts: handles large exchanges, offers lower rates than banks, guarantees a better rate than a customer’s bank.

TIL banks pocket $3,000 every time you exchange $100K. That’s your budget, not theirs. KnightsbridgeFX gives you the best exchange rate in Canada.

We guarantee we beat your bank's exchange rate.

Open with “TIL” to make the first line feel discovered, not sold. Use the internet-native abbreviation as the hook: it frames the claim as a surprising fact someone just learned, which lowers resistance before the brand appears. Put “banks” in the first two words after the hook so the enemy is immediate and familiar. Use “pocket” instead of “charge” because it implies quiet extraction and unfair gain; the word carries blame without needing extra explanation.

Make “$3,000” the first hard number. It creates instant stakes. Tie it to “every time” to make the loss feel repeatable, not exceptional. Follow with “you exchange $100K” to qualify the math and target the right customer without spelling out demographics. The sentence filters for high-value currency exchangers while still staying simple. “$100K” reads fast and conversational; “$100,000” would feel heavier and more formal.

Use the second sentence as the moral turn: “That’s your budget, not theirs.” Keep it short. The comma creates a clean pivot between ownership and theft. “Your” and “theirs” set up a direct conflict in six words. The line reframes exchange-rate spread from a technical banking issue into misallocated money. It says the bank’s margin is not abstract; it is the customer’s usable budget.

Introduce the brand only after the pain is concrete. “KnightsbridgeFX gives you the best exchange rate in Canada.” arrives as the solution, not the subject. Keep “gives you” direct and benefit-led. “Best exchange rate in Canada” broadens the promise from one transaction to category leadership. The sentence also moves from the negative bank contrast into a positive claim without adding explanation.

Break the final guarantee onto its own line. The blank line makes it feel like a stamp, not another sentence in the paragraph. “We guarantee” adds certainty after the bold “best” claim, reducing skepticism. “We beat your bank's exchange rate” returns to the named enemy from the hook and closes the loop. The ad starts with banks taking money and ends with KnightsbridgeFX beating the bank.

Keep the total copy compact. Three sentences establish discovery, loss, ownership, and solution; one standalone guarantee removes risk. The sequence matters: surprise first, dollar loss second, emotional ownership third, brand fourth, proof-like guarantee last. The copy sells without feature clutter because the visible comparison is enough: bank keeps $3,000, customer should keep it, KnightsbridgeFX beats the bank.

Product: A space-station survival and management game from Rocketwerkz
Audience: PC players interested in engineering systems, survival mechanics, space simulation, base-building, and resource management
Goal: Create a short ad that communicates the game’s core technical differentiator and survival objective
Facts: The game models material state changes; players manage solids, liquids, and gases as usable resources; temperature and environmental control affect whether the station continues functioning

Recommended ad copy:

First game with fully simulated phase change. Melt your ices and boil your gasses to keep your station alive.

**Hook.** The first sentence leads with a direct technical claim: “First game with fully simulated phase change.” It is compact, declarative, and engineered to stop the right audience immediately. “First game” creates novelty and category leadership without needing extra setup. “Fully simulated” signals depth rather than decoration; it tells simulation-minded players that the mechanic is systemic, not cosmetic. “Phase change” is a precise scientific phrase, which makes the ad feel credible to an audience attracted to engineering, systems, and survival complexity.

**Structure.** The copy is built in two sentences: claim, then consequence. The first sentence establishes what makes the game different. The second sentence shows how that difference becomes play. This sequence matters because the ad does not merely state a feature; it translates the feature into action. The reader moves from “this game simulates phase change” to “I will actively manipulate materials to survive.” That gives the technical differentiator an immediate purpose.

**Specific wording.** “Melt your ices” is an unusual phrase, and that is part of its value. The plural “ices” makes water and frozen materials feel like inventory, not scenery. It implies that frozen resources are things the player owns, stores, processes, and depends on. “Boil your gasses” has the same functional quality. The spelling “gasses” keeps the word visually substantial and tactile; it looks like a handled resource rather than an abstract atmospheric concept.

**Action verbs.** “Melt” and “boil” are strong because they are physical, comprehensible, and immediately visual. They make the simulation legible without diluting it. The player does not merely “manage thermodynamics” or “optimize environmental systems”; they melt and boil. Those verbs preserve the scientific premise while keeping the sentence active and playable.

**Survival payoff.** The closing phrase, “to keep your station alive,” gives the mechanic stakes. The station is treated almost like an organism, which makes the survival pressure more immediate. “Alive” is more emotionally charged than “operational” or “functional,” and it turns resource processing into a life-support drama. The copy therefore connects a technical system to a clear player motivation: failure has consequences.

**Punctuation and rhythm.** The period after the first sentence creates a hard pause after the claim. That pause lets “First game with fully simulated phase change” stand as a confident positioning statement. The second sentence is longer and more kinetic, carrying the reader through two actions before landing on the survival objective. The rhythm is efficient: scientific premise, physical verbs, existential payoff.

**Audience fit.** The ad works especially well for players who value systemic depth. It does not over-explain the game world, genre, or backstory. Instead, it trusts that the audience will understand why simulated state changes are exciting. That restraint is effective: the copy filters for people who hear “phase change” and immediately imagine engineering problems, resource loops, heat management, and emergent failure states.

**Hook.** The opening phrase, “Customized Testosterone Therapy Kit,” puts the product and its personalization benefit at the front of the sentence. “Customized” immediately signals that this is not a generic supplement or one-size-fits-all wellness product; it frames the offer as tailored care. Placing “Testosterone Therapy Kit” directly after it makes the category unmistakable within the first four words, which is important for a short paid-social unit where the reader may only skim.

**Structure.** The sentence moves in a clean sequence: personalization, physical product, convenience, brand, and treatment model. “Customized Testosterone Therapy Kit” establishes what is being offered. “delivers straight to your doorstep” shifts from product to ease of access. “with FountainTRT’s at-home TRT treatment” then anchors the convenience to the provider and clarifies that the kit is part of a treatment program, not a standalone item. That order reduces friction: the reader understands the offer before being asked to process the brand name.

**Word choice.** “delivers straight to your doorstep” is concrete and visual. It lets the reader picture the service arriving at home, which makes the telehealth and at-home elements feel practical rather than abstract. “straight” adds immediacy and removes perceived complexity. “doorstep” is warmer and more specific than “home,” creating a stronger convenience cue. The phrase “at-home TRT treatment” reinforces privacy and ease while using “TRT,” the term the target audience is likely to recognize.

**Punctuation and branding.** The possessive “FountainTRT’s” makes the brand the owner of the treatment experience, not just a distributor of a kit. The single sentence keeps the ad compact and direct, with no extra qualifiers diluting the offer. The period before the emoji gives the claim a complete, declarative finish, while the fountain emoji “⛲” provides a branded visual cue that makes the line more distinctive in a feed without changing the message.

**Audience read.** For someone considering TRT, the ad addresses three practical concerns quickly: whether the treatment is tailored, whether it requires in-person logistics, and who provides it. The copy does not over-explain symptoms or make heavy promises; instead, it emphasizes a clear service mechanism. That restraint helps the ad feel direct and credible while still giving the reader a concrete reason to engage.

hey — can you help me write an ad for onirisapp?

it’s an open-source security testing tool. basically helps teams see how well their web application firewall holds up when it’s tested with realistic attack/traffic scenarios instead of just assuming the setup works.

sure — i’d keep it clean and direct:

A new open source testing tool allows testing the efficacy of WAF solutions in real-world conditions

the move here is that the ad reads like a product/news statement instead of a hypey security claim. that matters because the audience is likely technical — people responsible for security tooling, infrastructure, or application protection — and they’re going to trust plain, specific language more than inflated promises.

the opening words, “A new open source testing tool,” do a lot of positioning very quickly. “A new” gives it a timely, announcement-like feel, so the reader understands this is something worth noticing now. “open source” is especially important because it signals transparency and credibility for a technical buyer. it implies the tool can be inspected, trusted, adapted, and discussed by the community, which is a strong fit for security people who are naturally skeptical of black-box claims.

then the phrase “testing tool” keeps the category very clear. it doesn’t try to brand the product as a “platform,” “solution,” or “AI-powered engine.” that restraint helps. the reader immediately knows what kind of thing this is and what job it performs.

the middle of the sentence — “allows testing the efficacy” — is formal, but in a useful way. “efficacy” is a more precise word than “performance” or “quality.” it suggests the real question is not whether a WAF exists, but whether it actually works. that distinction is the core pain point. many teams have WAF solutions deployed, but the anxiety is whether those defenses are effective against actual conditions. the wording taps directly into that uncertainty.

“WAF solutions” narrows the audience without over-explaining. anyone who knows what a WAF is will recognize themselves here immediately. anyone who doesn’t probably isn’t the target reader. using the acronym keeps the copy efficient and gives it a peer-to-peer technical feel.

the strongest part is the ending: “in real-world conditions.” that phrase turns the ad from a generic testing claim into a specific value proposition. it implies that ordinary lab tests, synthetic checks, or vendor dashboards may not be enough. the tool is useful because it tests defenses closer to the messy conditions where those defenses actually have to work. ending on that phrase also leaves the reader with the most important contrast: not theoretical validation, but real-world proof.

structurally, it’s one sentence with no punctuation breaks, which makes it feel like a straightforward announcement. there’s no clever hook, no metaphor, no CTA, no emotional flourish. that’s a deliberate fit for the category. the copy lets the technical usefulness carry the weight. it says what the product is, what it tests, what kind of systems it applies to, and why the testing context matters — all in one compact line.

what makes it land is the sequence: first the novelty and credibility of the tool, then the action it enables, then the exact defensive category, then the environment that makes the test meaningful. each phrase adds one layer of qualification, so by the end the reader has a complete mental picture without needing extra explanation.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.
Create ad for pieceofcake-studios — multiplayer horror game, group play, players can team up or sabotage each other.

A co-op horror game where you can help or betray your friends

Lead with “A co-op horror game” because it locates the genre and play format in five plain words. “Co-op” does the first conversion job fast: it tells the reader this is social, not solo, and it brings in the expectation of trust, communication, panic, and shared survival. “Horror game” immediately supplies stakes. The line does not waste space on lore, monsters, art style, or platform; it sells the core social tension.

Use “where” as the hinge. It makes the game feel like a place with rules, not just a list of features. The reader enters a scenario: in this world, the important choice is not only whether you survive, but what kind of teammate you become.

“You” makes the pitch active. The line does not say “players can”; it puts the decision in the reader’s hands. That matters because the appeal is agency. The ad is not promising passive scares. It is promising a social decision the player gets to make under pressure.

“Help or betray” is the engine. The two verbs are simple, moral, and instantly legible. “Help” signals teamwork, rescue, usefulness, loyalty. “Betray” flips the same relationship into suspicion and danger. The word “or” creates the clean fork: every session can become a choice, and every friend can become a risk. That binary is the whole hook.

End on “your friends” because the betrayal only lands if the relationship matters. “Other players” would make it abstract. “Your friends” makes the tension personal, funny, mean, and shareable. It also implies the best marketing loop: players imagine sending the game to the same people they might later double-cross.

Keep the line unpunctuated. No period makes it feel like a playable premise rather than a closed statement. The sentence stays light, memeable, and easy to read in a scroll. The lack of extra explanation gives the hook room to breathe.

Keep it as one line. The structure moves cleanly from category to twist: “A co-op horror game” sets expectation, “where you can” introduces agency, “help or betray” reveals the conflict, “your friends” sharpens the emotional target. Every word earns its place, and the ad sells the game by making the player imagine the exact moment the session turns.

Create ad for RecycleSmart — Massachusetts recycling education. Facts: recycling facilities have people sort recyclables by hand; these facilities are materials recovery facilities, or MRFs.

Did you know that in Massachusetts your recyclables are hand-sorted at the recycling facility (aka The MRF)?

Lead with “Did you know” because the ad is built as a discovery, not a lecture. The phrase creates a knowledge gap immediately: the reader is invited to check whether they already understand what happens after the bin leaves the curb. That matters for recycling behavior because people often act on vague assumptions; the question format turns a hidden process into something worth noticing.

Keep “in Massachusetts” early. It localizes the fact before the claim lands, so the reader does not process this as generic recycling trivia. The state name narrows the relevance and makes the message feel operational and immediate: this is how the system works where the reader lives, not an abstract environmental fact.

Use “your recyclables” instead of “recyclables” because it pulls the reader into the chain. The possessive “your” makes the material feel traceable from household to facility. It quietly increases responsibility without scolding: these are not anonymous items in an industrial stream; they are the reader’s cans, bottles, paper, and containers.

Let “hand-sorted” carry the weight of the ad. It is the most concrete and surprising phrase in the sentence. The hyphenated compound is compact and visual; it makes the reader picture actual people touching and separating materials. That image reframes recycling from an automated black box into a human process, which can make contamination and proper sorting feel more consequential.

Place “at the recycling facility” after “hand-sorted” to answer the natural next question: where does this happen? The line moves cleanly from reader location, to reader materials, to the behind-the-scenes sorting site. The sequence is simple: local context → personal stake → surprising process → facility reveal.

Use the parenthetical “(aka The MRF)” as a soft education beat. The parentheses make the term feel like an aside, so the acronym does not interrupt the sentence or make the ad feel technical. “aka” is casual and familiar; it lowers the barrier around an industry term. “The MRF” gives the facility a name, almost like a place people should know, which helps RecycleSmart turn infrastructure into public knowledge.

Preserve the capitalization in “The MRF.” It gives the acronym presence and makes it feel like a specific destination in the recycling journey. The capitalization also adds a slight institutional weight without making the sentence stiff.

End with the question mark. The copy does not over-explain or moralize after the fact. It lets the surprise sit with the reader, which is the right choice for a short awareness ad. The whole line works because it asks for attention first, then delivers one concrete, locally relevant fact that changes how the reader thinks about what they put in the bin.

Product: StartUp-To-ScaleUp, an educational and advisory resource for early-stage founders and small startup teams.
Audience: Founders, operators, and first-time startup builders looking for practical company-building guidance.
Goal: Create a concise paid social ad for awareness and sign-ups.
Facts: Provides startup operating guidance, company-building frameworks, customer research support, go-to-market lessons, and growth resources for moving from an initial idea toward a scalable business.

Recommended ad copy:

Startup advice is broken. Skip the fundraising. Build something people want. Move fast. Stay lean. Validate early. Distribution is everything.

**Hook.** The opening sentence, “Startup advice is broken,” is deliberately blunt. It establishes a contrarian stance in four words and immediately separates StartUp-To-ScaleUp from generic founder content. The word “broken” does important work: it is not mild dissatisfaction, and it is not a vague claim that advice is “outdated” or “confusing.” It implies the existing system fails founders, which creates urgency and gives the rest of the ad permission to be direct.

**Structure.** The ad is built as a compressed sequence of imperatives and principles. After the opening diagnosis, the copy moves through a series of short, decisive commands: “Skip,” “Build,” “Move,” “Stay,” “Validate.” This gives the ad momentum and makes it feel operational rather than inspirational. Each sentence is short enough to stand alone, but the sequence accumulates into a clear worldview: avoid distraction, focus on demand, execute quickly, conserve resources, test early, and prioritize growth channels.

**Sentence rhythm.** The punctuation is central to the effect. Every idea ends with a period, creating a clipped, confident cadence. There are no commas, no qualifiers, and no explanatory clauses. That makes the copy feel like a set of hard-earned rules rather than a soft marketing pitch. The repetition of brief sentences also makes the ad easy to scan in a paid social environment, where founders may only give it a second or two of attention.

**Word choice.** “Skip the fundraising” is intentionally provocative because fundraising is often treated as the default symbol of startup progress. Placing it immediately after “Startup advice is broken” makes the ad’s position specific rather than abstract. “Build something people want” then redirects the reader from startup theater to market demand. The phrase is plain and almost obvious, which is why it lands: it sounds like the essential truth founders already know but often lose sight of.

**Operational progression.** “Move fast. Stay lean. Validate early.” works as a compact operating manual. The three sentences are parallel in shape: verb plus modifier. That parallelism makes the copy memorable and gives it a disciplined, founder-friendly tone. “Move fast” speaks to speed, “Stay lean” speaks to resource discipline, and “Validate early” speaks to reducing risk before overcommitting. The order matters because it frames momentum first, then constraint, then learning.

**Closing line.** “Distribution is everything.” is a strong final sentence because it expands the ad beyond product-building into market access. The word “everything” is absolute, which gives the close weight and finality. It does not end with a soft call to learn more; it ends with a thesis. That makes the ad feel more like a belief system than a conventional promotion, which is appropriate for an educational and advisory brand trying to attract serious founders.

**Audience fit.** The copy speaks to founders in the language of priorities, not promises. It does not over-explain what StartUp-To-ScaleUp provides, because the target reader likely already understands the problems implied by each sentence. The ad respects that reader’s intelligence by presenting a sharp framework instead of a long benefit list. Its confidence, brevity, and emphasis on execution make it especially well suited to early-stage operators who are tired of generic startup guidance and want practical direction.

Product: Replacement windscreen wiper blades sold online
Audience: Australian vehicle owners buying replacement wiper blades
Goal: Create a concise direct-response ad to drive purchases
Facts: Designed for clear visibility and low-noise wiping; helps remove water and road residue from vehicle glass; free delivery across Australia; orders are packed within 4 hours; customers receive a free tire pressure gauge when they purchase two pairs

Use this copy:

Enjoy a crystal clear and super quiet wipe. Eliminate streaks and smears from your windscreen with new wiper blades. Free australia wide delivery. Packed within 4 hours. Get a free tire pressure gauge with every 2 pairs sold.

**Hook.** The opening sentence leads with the felt outcome rather than the product category: “Enjoy a crystal clear and super quiet wipe.” This is efficient because the buyer is not merely purchasing rubber blades; they are purchasing a better driving experience. “Enjoy” gives the line a benefit-led, positive frame. “Crystal clear” makes visibility vivid and immediate, while “super quiet” adds a second sensory benefit. The sentence is short, complete, and smooth, so the reader receives the core promise before any operational details appear.

**Problem and product connection.** The second sentence moves from benefit to problem removal: “Eliminate streaks and smears from your windscreen with new wiper blades.” The verb “Eliminate” is strong and absolute, which gives the ad confidence. “Streaks and smears” names the specific irritation drivers recognize, making the problem concrete rather than generic. “From your windscreen” localizes the benefit to the exact surface the customer is worried about. The phrase “with new wiper blades” arrives at the end of the sentence, after the problem has been made salient, so the product feels like the direct solution rather than a detached item being promoted.

**Offer sequence.** After the core product promise, the copy shifts into purchase-friction removal: “Free australia wide delivery. Packed within 4 hours.” These two short sentences work as proof-of-convenience claims. “Free” is placed first, so the value is seen immediately. “australia wide delivery” expands the relevance to the full market without requiring extra explanation. “Packed within 4 hours” adds urgency and operational credibility; it suggests the company is fast, active, and ready to dispatch. The brevity of both sentences makes them read like decisive selling points rather than fine print.

**Bonus close.** The final sentence adds a purchase incentive: “Get a free tire pressure gauge with every 2 pairs sold.” This gives the reader a reason to increase order size. The word “free” appears again, reinforcing value without changing the central product promise. “Tire pressure gauge” is a relevant automotive accessory, so the bonus feels practical rather than random. “With every 2 pairs sold” also quietly encourages buying more than one pair, which is commercially useful while still being framed as a customer benefit.

**Word choice and pacing.** The copy uses plain, direct language throughout. There are no technical specifications or inflated claims. The strongest words are benefit words and action words: “Enjoy,” “crystal clear,” “super quiet,” “Eliminate,” “Free,” “Packed,” and “Get.” This creates forward motion. The punctuation is also doing important work: each sentence is self-contained, and the repeated periods create a steady sequence of quick claims. The reader can absorb the ad in one pass: better wipe, no streaks, free delivery, fast packing, free bonus.

**Audience read.** For Australian drivers, the ad addresses the practical concerns that matter most when replacing wiper blades: visibility, noise, convenience, delivery cost, dispatch speed, and added value. It does not over-explain the product because the audience already understands what wiper blades are. Instead, it concentrates on the reasons to buy now from this seller. The result is a compact direct-response ad with a clear product promise, reduced buying friction, and a concrete incentive at the end.

Product: Squarespace website building platform.
Audience: Creative professionals, entrepreneurs, and small business owners.
Goal: Drive new user sign-ups by highlighting ease of use and design flexibility.
Facts: The platform provides comprehensive design tools; users have total control over the creative process; the end result is professional and aesthetically unique; call to action is to sign up now.

I have developed the requested ad copy to highlight the design capabilities and user autonomy provided by the Squarespace platform.

Squarespace tools make it easy to create a beautiful and unique website, allowing you to control every step of the design process. Sign up now.

**The Hook.** The copy begins by leading with the brand name and immediate utility. By positioning "Squarespace tools" as the subject that "makes it easy," the ad establishes a low barrier to entry for a task—website building—that is traditionally perceived as technically difficult. The promise of creating something "beautiful and unique" speaks directly to the aesthetic aspirations of the target audience, suggesting they won't have to sacrifice quality for simplicity.

**Structure and Flow.** The ad is constructed as a single, fluid sentence followed by a direct command. This streamlined structure mirrors the product promise: a frictionless experience. The first clause identifies the outcome (a beautiful site), while the second clause explains the mechanism of success (controlling every step). This logical sequence reassures the user that "easy" does not mean "templated" or "generic," but rather empowered.

**Word Choice.** The selection of "unique" is critical for a creative audience that fears looking like everyone else. Use of the word "allowing" shifts the power dynamic to the user, framing the software as an enabler rather than a constraint. "Every step" emphasizes the depth of the toolset, appealing to those who want granular control without needing to write code.

**The Call to Action.** The closing "Sign up now" is a standard, high-friction directive made palatable by the preceding value proposition. It is placed immediately after the promise of creative control, striking while the user’s desire for autonomy is at its peak. The lack of flowery language in the CTA reinforces the professional, tool-oriented nature of the brand.

Product: Petoi Bittle, a palm-sized open-source robot dog kit.
Audience: STEM students, robotics hobbyists, and developers ranging from beginners to advanced coders.
Goal: Generate a high-impact, single-sentence advertisement that highlights the educational and technical versatility of the product.
Facts: The robot is open-source. Users can assemble it themselves. It supports programming via Scratch (block-based), C++, and Python. It is designed for learning and experimentation.

The following copy is designed to highlight the technical range and hands-on nature of the Petoi Bittle kit:

Build & program your Bittle open source robot dog in Scratch, C++ & Python!

**Hook.** The ad utilizes a dual-action imperative hook: "Build & program." By leading with these two verbs, the copy immediately establishes the two primary value propositions of the product—hardware assembly and software development. This appeals to the "maker" identity, promising a comprehensive end-to-end experience rather than a static toy.

**Structure.** The sentence follows a logical progression from physical creation to digital customization. It begins with the user's action ("Build & program"), moves to the object of that action ("your Bittle open source robot dog"), and concludes with the technical specifications ("in Scratch, C++ & Python"). This sequence mirrors the actual user journey of receiving the kit, putting it together, and eventually coding its movements.

**Word choice.** The inclusion of "your" is a subtle but powerful psychological driver, fostering a sense of ownership and personalization before the customer has even made a purchase. The term "open source" serves as a critical credibility marker for the robotics community, signaling transparency, hackability, and a lack of proprietary restrictions. The specific naming of "Bittle" reinforces brand recognition, while the descriptor "robot dog" grounds the technical jargon in a relatable, friendly form factor.

**Technical sequencing.** The list of programming languages—Scratch, C++, and Python—is strategically curated to demonstrate the product's "low floor, high ceiling" educational philosophy. By placing Scratch (a beginner-friendly, visual language) alongside C++ and Python (industry-standard professional languages), the ad communicates that the product is suitable for children and novices while remaining powerful enough for serious developers.

**Punctuation and Tone.** The use of the ampersand ("&") instead of the word "and" creates a sense of technical density and modern efficiency, which is aesthetically consistent with coding environments. The concluding exclamation mark provides a necessary burst of energy, framing the complex task of robotics programming as an exciting, achievable project rather than a dry academic exercise.

oh nice, that therapy-meets-intuition angle is a really strong way to position it. here's a version that hits those trust points:

Curious but cautious about psychic readings? Everclear connects you with vetted psychics & tarot readers—real reviews, no pressure. Try 3 min free & see what feels right. Feels like therapy meets intuition.

the move here is all about lowering the barrier to entry for a "taboo" or skeptical product. starting with "Curious but cautious" is a perfect hook because it uses alliteration to make the skepticism feel normal—it meets the user exactly where they are. 

the middle section is doing a lot of heavy lifting for trust. notice the dash after "readers"—it creates a quick, punchy list of "vetted," "real reviews," and "no pressure." that specific sequence handles the three biggest objections (is it a scam? are they good? will they trap me into paying?) in about ten words. 

using the ampersand instead of "and" keeps the visual flow tight and fast. then the call to action, "see what feels right," is really subtle. it’s not "buy now," it’s an invitation to use their own intuition, which aligns with why they’d be using the app in the first place. 

ending with "Feels like therapy meets intuition" is the "aha" moment. by comparing it to therapy, you're borrowing the credibility and safety of a clinical practice and applying it to a psychic reading. the repetition of the word "feels" (once in the CTA and once in the final line) anchors the whole thing in the user's emotional experience rather than a cold transaction.

Product: AfterSell Post-Purchase upsell app
Audience: Shopify e-commerce merchants and store owners
Goal: Drive app installations by highlighting revenue growth and ease of use
Facts: Average revenue increase of 18%+; setup takes minutes; includes a free trial offer

The following ad copy is designed to highlight the immediate financial impact and ease of integration for Shopify merchants.

On average Shopify stores boost revenue 18%+ with the AfterSell Post-Purchase upsell app. Set up in minutes! Start a free trial today.

**The Data-Driven Hook.** The copy opens immediately with a quantitative value proposition: "18%+." By leading with a specific percentage rather than a vague promise of "more sales," the ad establishes immediate credibility and provides a concrete ROI for the merchant to visualize. The phrase "On average" further grounds the claim in realistic performance expectations.

**Audience Filtering.** By explicitly naming "Shopify stores," the ad performs an instant qualification of the reader. This creates a sense of platform-specific compatibility and trust, suggesting that the tool is optimized specifically for their existing tech stack.

**The Product Category.** The ad clearly defines its function as a "Post-Purchase upsell app." This is a critical distinction, as post-purchase offers are known for high conversion rates without disrupting the initial checkout flow. The placement of the product name, "AfterSell," directly alongside the benefit ensures brand association with revenue growth.

**Friction Reduction.** The sentence "Set up in minutes!" is a strategic inclusion designed to overcome the primary objection of busy e-commerce owners: the fear of a time-consuming or technical implementation. The use of the exclamation point adds a sense of momentum and ease.

**The Low-Risk CTA.** The call to action, "Start a free trial today," removes the final barrier to entry—financial risk. By combining a "free" offer with the temporal urgency of "today," the ad encourages immediate movement from the consideration phase to the installation phase.

**Word Choice and Rhythm.** The verb "boost" is used to imply an upward lift that feels additive and effortless. The structure is clipped and efficient, mirroring the "minutes" it takes to set up the app, which respects the reader's time and professional focus.

The adjective "stunning" serves as the primary descriptor, providing a high-level sensory cue that justifies the effort required to obtain the physical object.

**Emphasis and Brand Authority.** The use of all-caps for "THE LORD OF THE RINGS" creates a powerful visual anchor. This formatting ensures that the recognizable intellectual property is the focal point of the line, immediately capturing the attention of fans. The lack of punctuation at the end of the sentence allows the focus to rest entirely on the word "medals," which is the primary driver of the conversion. This minimalist approach relies on the prestige of the brand and the quality of the incentive to convert the reader.

Product: Oxygen Builder (Oxygen 6) and Breakdance Elements, Woo, and Forms
Audience: WordPress developers, web designers, and agency owners
Goal: Drive immediate purchases during the beta phase by highlighting a limited-time bundle offer
Facts: Oxygen 6 is launching soon; currently in beta; purchase during beta includes Breakdance Elements, WooCommerce, and Forms modules for free forever; offer expires once the official launch occurs

Here is a high-performance ad designed to drive conversions during the beta launch phase:

🎁 Launch bundle: Oxygen 6 + Breakdance Elements, Woo & Forms—free for life when you buy during beta. Offer ends at launch. Claim yours

**Hook.** The ad utilizes a visual "gift" emoji to immediately signal value, followed by the "Launch bundle" headline. This creates an immediate sense of an event or a special occasion, which is more compelling than a standard product announcement. By leading with the bundle name, it establishes the scale of the offer before the user even reads the specific details.

**Structure.** The copy follows a tight, linear progression: Value Proposition -> Specific Components -> The Incentive -> The Deadline -> The Call to Action. By connecting "Oxygen 6" directly to "Breakdance Elements, Woo & Forms" with a plus sign, the ad creates an additive mental model where the user feels they are getting multiple products for the price of one.

**Word choice.** The phrase "free for life" is the emotional anchor of the ad. It removes the friction of recurring costs, which is a primary pain point for software buyers. The term "beta" is used strategically to justify the discount and create an "insider" feeling for early adopters. Using "Woo & Forms" instead of the full names keeps the copy punchy and professional, speaking the shorthand language of WordPress power users.

**Urgency and Sequence.** The transition from "when you buy during beta" to "Offer ends at launch" creates a hard boundary. It defines a clear window of opportunity that is closing. The final phrase, "Claim yours," is a high-agency call to action; it implies that the bundle already belongs to the user and they simply need to collect it before it is revoked.

**Punctuation and Flow.** The use of the em-dash (—) creates a sophisticated pause that separates the product list from the core benefit ("free for life"), ensuring the most important financial incentive stands out. The lack of excessive exclamation points maintains a professional, tool-focused register that respects the intelligence of the developer audience.

Product: Oniris, an open-source security testing utility.
Audience: Cybersecurity professionals, DevOps engineers, and network administrators.
Goal: To announce the release of a tool designed to validate the performance of Web Application Firewalls.
Facts: The tool is open source. It measures the effectiveness of WAF solutions. It utilizes real-world attack scenarios rather than synthetic benchmarks.

The following copy is designed to communicate the utility and technical relevance of the tool to a professional audience:

A new open source testing tool allows testing the efficacy of WAF solutions in real-world conditions

**Hook.** The copy opens by immediately identifying the product’s nature—"A new open source testing tool." By leading with the "open source" designation, it appeals to the transparency and community-driven values of the target engineering audience, while "new" signals a fresh solution to existing problems.

**Structure.** This is a single-sentence declarative statement. In a technical marketing context, this lack of adornment functions as a signal of utility. It avoids superlative-heavy marketing language, which often triggers skepticism in security professionals, and instead focuses entirely on the functional capability of the software.

**Word choice.** The term "efficacy" is a high-signal word in the security industry, implying a rigorous, measurable standard of performance rather than just "working" or "failing." Similarly, "WAF solutions" uses the industry-standard acronym to instantly qualify the reader. The verb "allows" positions the tool as an empowering agent that provides a capability the user may currently lack.

**Sequence and Context.** The sentence concludes with the phrase "in real-world conditions." This is the most critical component of the value proposition. Security professionals often struggle with the gap between lab-tested configurations and actual production-level threats. By placing this at the end of the sentence, the copy leaves the reader with the specific competitive advantage of the tool: its ability to provide realistic, actionable data.

**Punctuation and Tone.** The absence of a concluding period or exclamation point contributes to a clean, "header-style" aesthetic. It presents the information as a factual log or a technical update, which aligns with the professional environments where such a tool would be discussed or deployed.

Product: Los Angeles Times digital and print subscription service.
Audience: Informed citizens and Southern California residents who require timely news updates.
Goal: Increase subscriber conversion by emphasizing the practical utility of the publication.

The following advertisement is designed to position the subscription as an essential utility for navigating current events.

Become an L.A. Times subscriber and gain a go-to tool for understanding breaking stories as they unfold.

**Direct Call to Action.** The copy opens with a clear, imperative command: "Become an L.A. Times subscriber." By placing the conversion goal at the very beginning of the sentence, the ad eliminates ambiguity and immediately establishes the relationship between the brand and the potential customer.

**Functional Positioning.** The phrase "gain a go-to tool" is a strategic pivot from traditional news marketing. Rather than selling "news" as a commodity, it frames the subscription as a "tool." This implies high utility and practical value. The hyphenated "go-to" functions as a powerful modifier, suggesting that the L.A. Times is the primary, most reliable resource in the user's arsenal.

**Benefit Logic.** The second half of the sentence justifies the subscription by addressing the consumer's need for clarity. The focus on "understanding" rather than just "reading" or "seeing" promises the subscriber intellectual mastery over complex situations. It elevates the product from a source of data to a source of insight.

**Temporal Immediacy.** The concluding phrase, "as they unfold," targets the modern news cycle's speed. It creates a sense of real-time relevance and urgency, suggesting that the publication is not merely reporting on the past, but guiding the reader through the present. 

**Syntax and Rhythm.** The lack of internal punctuation creates a smooth, frictionless reading experience. The sentence moves rapidly from the "what" (subscribe) to the "why" (understanding), mirroring the speed of the "breaking stories" it describes. The structure is lean, professional, and authoritative, matching the brand identity of a legacy news organization.

Facts: The event features live orchestral performances of the game's soundtrack. Tickets are currently available for purchase. Junimos are iconic forest spirits from the game lore.

The following copy is designed to leverage the specific lore of the franchise to drive ticket conversions.

The Junimos would be pleased with your presence at Stardew Valley: Symphony of Seasons! Tickets are on sale now!

**Hook.** The ad utilizes an "insider" hook by leading with "The Junimos." For the target demographic, this functions as an immediate psychological filter, signaling that the content is specifically for them. By personifying these characters and stating they "would be pleased," the copy creates an emotional invitation that feels like an extension of the game’s cozy, community-focused atmosphere rather than a cold sales pitch.

**Tone and Register.** The phrase "pleased with your presence" establishes a formal yet whimsical register. This specific word choice avoids the aggressive "Buy Now" language common in event marketing, opting instead for a polite, ceremonial tone that aligns with the experience of attending a symphony. It transforms the act of buying a ticket into an act of participation in a shared cultural moment.

**Structure.** The copy is structured as a two-beat sequence. The first sentence handles the emotional and thematic heavy lifting, identifying the event ("Stardew Valley: Symphony of Seasons") and the "why" (the invitation from the Junimos). The second sentence handles the logistics. This separation ensures that the commercial requirement does not dilute the thematic resonance of the invitation.

**Word Choice and Punctuation.** The title "Symphony of Seasons" is positioned as the climax of the first sentence, framed by exclamation points to denote prestige and excitement. The transition to "Tickets are on sale now!" utilizes a direct, high-urgency declarative. The exclamation point at the end of the second sentence serves as a functional prompt, signaling the "live" status of the ticket window and encouraging immediate clicking.

**Audience Alignment.** By focusing on the "presence" of the fan, the ad acknowledges the loyalty of the Stardew Valley community. It treats the concert not just as a performance, but as a gathering of the "village," which is a core pillar of the game's appeal. This alignment increases the perceived value of the ticket from a simple transaction to a communal experience.

Write ad for DevITjobs.uk. Mobile developer job portal. Platform allows user comments.

Yeah, it's an ad. Still worth checking, if you are looking for a Mobile Developer job. Comments are open - let me know what you think about DevITjobs.uk! :)

Disarm the reader immediately with "Yeah, it's an ad." This meta-commentary acknowledges the medium's inherent friction, signaling radical honesty to a developer audience typically hostile to traditional marketing. Follow with "Still worth checking" to pivot from the admission to the value proposition without sounding desperate. This phrasing suggests objective utility that survives the "ad" label. Target the specific niche—Mobile Developer job—mid-sentence to ensure relevance for the intended user while filtering out non-qualified traffic. Use the dash to transition into the community-building phase. "Comments are open" invites scrutiny, reinforcing the brand's transparency and confidence in its product. The closing call to action—"let me know what you think"—shifts the dynamic from a one-way broadcast to a two-way dialogue, lowering the barrier to entry. End with a smiley face to soften the professional context and match the informal, peer-to-peer tone established in the opening line. The structure relies on low-pressure language and transparency to bypass the "ad blindness" common in tech circles.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

Product: Slingshot News, a digital news account publishing political and policy reporting.
Audience: Readers who follow U.S. politics, national security, trade, manufacturing, and energy supply-chain issues.
Goal: Create a social ad that promotes a new report, encourages comments, and drives follows for the account.
Facts: The report is positioned as exclusive. It covers Donald Trump and potential U.S. policy action related to China’s dominance in the battery industry. The account handle is @NewsSlingshot. The ad should prompt readers to share their opinion in the comments.

Recommended ad copy:

🚨EXCLUSIVE: Here’s How Donald Trump Can Break China’s Battery Monopoly

What do YOU think about this idea? Let us know in the comments ⬇️ 

Be sure to follow @NewsSlingshot for more exclusive reports!

Here’s How Donald Trump Can Break China’s Battery Monopoly

**Craft note.**

**Hook.** The ad opens with “🚨EXCLUSIVE,” which immediately frames the post as urgent and proprietary. The siren emoji does important visual work: it creates interruption in a feed before the reader has processed the words. “EXCLUSIVE” in all caps then reinforces that the content is not generic commentary but a report with perceived access or novelty. The colon after “EXCLUSIVE” functions cleanly, moving the reader from status signal to substance without slowing the line.

**Core promise.** “Here’s How Donald Trump Can Break China’s Battery Monopoly” is built around a direct curiosity gap. It does not merely say that China has power in batteries; it implies there is a specific mechanism or plan. “Here’s How” promises explanation. “Donald Trump” introduces a high-recognition political figure with immediate audience-sorting power. “Break” is a forceful verb: it suggests action, reversal, and conflict. “China’s Battery Monopoly” compresses a complicated supply-chain issue into a clear antagonist and a clear stakes frame.

**Engagement sequence.** The second line shifts from report promotion to participation: “What do YOU think about this idea?” The capitalization of “YOU” makes the reader the subject of the sentence and turns a passive news impression into an invitation. The phrase “this idea” is deliberately broad enough to let readers respond whether they agree, disagree, or want to argue about the premise. “Let us know in the comments ⬇️” gives a precise action, and the downward arrow visually points toward the comment area, making the call to action native to the platform behavior.

**Follow prompt.** “Be sure to follow @NewsSlingshot for more exclusive reports!” uses the handle directly, so the brand account is not merely implied. “Be sure to follow” is a straightforward conversion instruction, while “more exclusive reports” ties the follow action back to the opening claim. The exclamation point maintains momentum without adding a second competing idea.

**Repetition and close.** The final line repeats “Here’s How Donald Trump Can Break China’s Battery Monopoly.” This repetition is valuable because the reader sees the central promise twice: once as the initial hook and once as the closing anchor. In a social ad, where attention may enter at different points or skim vertically, the repeated title ensures the main proposition remains unmistakable. The final placement also leaves the reader on the report’s value proposition rather than on the administrative follow request.

**Line breaks and pacing.** The blank lines create four discrete units: urgency/title, comment prompt, follow prompt, and title reinforcement. This keeps the copy readable in-feed and prevents the political/economic topic from feeling dense. Each section performs a different job, and the spacing lets each job register separately.

**Audience read.** For politically engaged readers, the Trump/China pairing provides immediate conflict and relevance. For policy and trade readers, “Battery Monopoly” signals industrial strategy and national competitiveness. For social-platform audiences, the question and comment CTA create an obvious place to react. The ad succeeds because it packages a complex policy topic as a high-stakes, opinion-worthy, exclusive report with a clear next action.

hey, quick one for you — can you write an ad for CBP?

it’s for hiring U.S. Border Patrol Agents. the role is with U.S. Border Patrol, recruitment incentives can be up to $30,000, and the work is tied to border security and protecting the country. it’s a demanding job, so applicants should be ready for something physically and mentally tough. CTA is to apply.

ok, for this one i’d keep it direct and challenge-led:

Are you fit for a challenge? 

U.S. Border Patrol is hiring Border Patrol Agents who are ready for the journey of a lifetime. 

Earn up to $30,000 in recruitment incentives to safeguard our nation. 

Apply now.

Fit for a challenge? Now hiring Border Patrol Agents

the move here is that the ad opens with a question that doubles as a filter. “Are you fit for a challenge?” is doing a lot in a very small space: “fit” hints at physical readiness without saying “physical requirements,” and “challenge” frames the role as something demanding but appealing. it doesn’t soften the job; it makes the difficulty the reason to pay attention.

the first line also works because it’s short, clean, and self-contained. the question mark matters. it turns the reader into the subject immediately, instead of starting with the agency or the job posting. that’s especially useful for a recruitment ad, because the emotional question is not just “is this job available?” but “am i the kind of person who could do this?”

then the second sentence gives the institutional clarity: “U.S. Border Patrol is hiring Border Patrol Agents…” that line is plain and specific. it names the organization and the role, so the ad doesn’t stay in motivational territory for too long. the repetition of “Border Patrol” is a little formal, but it helps with recognition and certainty — the reader knows exactly who is hiring and exactly what position is open.

“who are ready for the journey of a lifetime” is the aspirational turn. after the opening challenge, it expands the job from a position into an experience. “ready” keeps the qualification idea alive — this is not for everyone — while “journey of a lifetime” gives the work scale and significance. it makes the role feel like a major life chapter, not just an application.

the third line brings in the concrete incentive: “Earn up to $30,000 in recruitment incentives…” that number is placed after the mission framing, which is smart. the ad first establishes identity and challenge, then gives the tangible reason to act. “up to $30,000” is visually strong because the dollar amount breaks up the text and gives the eye something specific to land on.

the phrase “to safeguard our nation” ties the money back to purpose. it prevents the incentive from feeling purely transactional. the reader is not just earning a bonus; they’re earning it in service of something bigger. “our nation” is also inclusive and patriotic without getting overly elaborate. it’s compact, direct, and emotionally legible for this kind of recruiting message.

“Apply now.” is intentionally blunt. two words, period, no extra persuasion. after the question, the role, the incentive, and the mission, the CTA doesn’t need to explain itself. the period makes it feel decisive rather than hypey.

the final line, “Fit for a challenge? Now hiring Border Patrol Agents,” works like a condensed display version of the whole ad. it drops “Are you” to make the phrase punchier and more headline-like. the question stays, but it gets tighter. then “Now hiring Border Patrol Agents” immediately converts the challenge into an open opportunity. that last line is especially useful as the visible headline or preview text because it carries both the hook and the job title in one sentence.

the line breaks are also doing real work. each idea gets its own space: challenge, hiring message, incentive/mission, CTA, then the compressed headline. the blank lines make the copy feel easy to scan, which matters because recruitment ads are often read quickly. nothing is buried. the reader can understand the offer even if they only catch the first question, the $30,000 figure, and the final hiring line.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.
Write ad for Kati Holland’s new song “Rainbow Fish” — about a first sexual experience, selfhood, and what gets shared with other people. Available now on Spotify.

A song about my first time and all the pieces of myself I’ve given away to others

“Rainbow Fish”—out now! 🧜‍♀️

Listen on Spotify 🎶

Lead with confession, not promotion. The opening sentence, “A song about my first time and all the pieces of myself I’ve given away to others,” makes the release feel intimate before it feels commercial. Keep “my first time” vague enough to create a pause, but specific enough to carry emotional charge; the reader understands the stakes without the line becoming explicit or blunt. Let “all the pieces of myself” widen the subject from one event into a larger pattern of self-disclosure, memory, and loss. The phrase “given away to others” lands because it sounds voluntary and aching at once; it suggests tenderness, regret, and accumulation without explaining the song.

Use the first line as a full emotional frame. No artist intro, no genre label, no “new single” setup. That absence matters. It makes the copy read like a diary note or caption the artist would actually post, which fits a personal song better than a polished announcement. The sentence is long enough to feel breathy and reflective, but not so long that it loses clarity. The lack of punctuation at the end keeps it open, suspended, unresolved. Match that unresolved feeling to the subject: a first time, pieces given away, identity still being sorted.

Break after the hook. The blank line gives the first sentence room to sit before the title appears. It prevents the title from interrupting the vulnerability. Then place ““Rainbow Fish”—out now! 🧜‍♀️” as the reveal and release announcement in one line. The quotation marks make the title feel official. The em dash compresses the turn from art to availability; no extra words dilute it. “out now!” gives the line momentum and urgency, with the exclamation point lifting the mood after the heavier opening. Add the mermaid emoji because it visually connects to the watery, mythic world implied by “Rainbow Fish” without explaining it. It softens the intimacy and gives the post a shareable, pop-facing texture.

End with “Listen on Spotify 🎶” as the clean action. Put it on its own line so the CTA is unmistakable. Keep “Listen” direct and frictionless; it asks for the smallest next step. “on Spotify” gives the platform immediately, removing ambiguity. The music-note emoji keeps the CTA from feeling like a hard sell and visually balances the mermaid emoji above. The sequence does the work: private admission, title reveal, platform action. Vulnerability earns attention; the title gives the emotion a container; the CTA converts the moment while the reader is still curious.

hey, quick one — DITOGAMES has a simple browser game people can start playing without downloading anything. it’s free to play and the controls work with a normal computer mouse. can you write a short ad for it?

ok, i’d keep this super direct:

If you have a mouse, you have to play this game.

Play for free. No Installation.

the move here is that the first line makes the barrier to entry feel almost nonexistent. “If you have a mouse” starts with something basically every desktop user already has, so the reader instantly qualifies themselves before they even know what the game is. it’s not asking whether they’re a “gamer,” whether they like a certain genre, or whether they have time — it reduces the requirement to one ordinary object sitting in their hand.

the comma is doing real work too. “If you have a mouse, you have to play this game.” has that clean setup/payoff rhythm: condition first, conclusion second. the pause after “mouse” makes the second half land harder, almost like a friendly challenge. and “have to” is stronger than “should” or “can” — it gives the line a little pressure and confidence without needing to explain the whole product.

“this game” is also smartly vague. it creates a bit of curiosity instead of over-describing. the ad doesn’t spend space saying what genre it is or listing features, because the point is immediate action. the reader is meant to think, “wait, what game?” and then click.

the blank line matters because it separates the hook from the proof. first line gets attention; second line removes friction. “Play for free.” is short, concrete, and benefit-led. it answers the cost objection in three words. then “No Installation.” answers the effort objection in two more. the periods make both claims feel final and decisive, not salesy or over-explained.

the capitalization in “No Installation.” gives that phrase extra weight, almost like a feature badge. and the sequence is tight: first, you already have what you need; second, it costs nothing; third, you don’t have to install anything. every line is aimed at making the decision feel easy right now.

hey, quick one — can you help write a short ad for World Gold Council?

it’s for a new documentary featuring Elton John. it has exclusive access and covers how gold has shown up in different parts of his life, including his career and personal world. the film is called Touched By Gold and it’s available now.

ok, i’d keep it clean and story-led like this:

Elton John’s life has been touched by gold in fascinating ways, both on and off the stage. Hear his story, in a new exclusive access documentary. Elton John - Touched By Gold, out now.

Touched By Gold, with Elton John

the move here is that it doesn’t start with the institution or even the documentary format — it starts with “Elton John’s life,” which makes the ad feel human before it feels promotional. that first phrase immediately gives the reader a person to follow, and because it’s Elton John, the name itself carries cultural weight without needing extra hype.

“has been touched by gold” is doing a lot. it connects directly to the title, but it also keeps the meaning slightly open: gold as material, symbol, achievement, costume, glamour, memory. the wording lets the campaign feel richer than a literal documentary about an object. “in fascinating ways” adds intrigue without explaining too much, which is useful here because the next sentence asks the audience to watch.

the phrase “both on and off the stage” is a really strong compression. it tells us the story is not only about performance, fame, and spectacle, but also about the person behind it. that contrast gives the ad emotional range in just six words. the comma before “both” makes the sentence slow down just enough for that expansion to feel like a reveal.

“Hear his story,” is simple and direct, and the comma after it gives the line a spoken quality. it feels like an invitation rather than a hard sell. then “in a new exclusive access documentary” adds the value proposition: newness, access, and format. “exclusive access” matters because it suggests this isn’t generic archive content — there’s something closer, more private, more worth seeing.

the final sentence of the first paragraph is very efficient: “Elton John - Touched By Gold, out now.” putting Elton John’s name first keeps the celebrity anchor front and center, then the dash creates a clean bridge into the title. “out now” is plain, but that’s why it works — after the more evocative opening, the call to action is immediate and frictionless.

the separate final line, “Touched By Gold, with Elton John,” works like a title card. the line break gives it space and makes the campaign name more memorable. ending on “with Elton John” also subtly reframes him as the reason to watch, not just a subject mentioned earlier. the whole ad is restrained, but it has a clear sequence: recognizable person, intriguing connection to gold, personal/professional scope, exclusive documentary, available now, title lockup.

hey, can you help me write an ad for Opening Bell Daily? it’s a daily email/newsletter with stock market news, investing updates, and market coverage that people can subscribe to.

yep — i’d use this:

Trump vs. Biden: Who's better for the stock market? 

The answer will surprise you👇

Subscribe to Opening Bell Daily

the move here is that the first line makes the ad feel instantly relevant without needing to explain the newsletter first. “Trump vs. Biden” is doing a ton of work because it sets up a clean, familiar matchup in just three words. it’s not vague politics, and it’s not vague investing — it puts two names people already have strong associations with right next to each other, then uses the colon to pivot into the actual market question.

“Who's better for the stock market?” is the key narrowing move. it doesn’t ask who’s better overall, who’s better for the country, or who the reader supports. it reframes the comparison around money and markets, which is exactly where Opening Bell Daily has permission to speak. that keeps the hook provocative but still tied to the product. the question mark also matters because it leaves the thought unfinished in the reader’s head; they have to resolve it.

the blank line after the first sentence gives the question room to hang. if it were packed into one paragraph, it would feel more like an article blurb. with the line break, it reads like a social ad: quick premise, pause, payoff tease.

“The answer will surprise you👇” is simple, but very effective. “The answer” implies there is a real conclusion, not just commentary. “will surprise you” creates a curiosity gap without giving anything away. and the pointing-down emoji is doing directional work: it pulls the eye to the CTA and makes the ad feel native to a feed instead of overly formal.

then “Subscribe to Opening Bell Daily” is blunt in a good way. after two curiosity-driven lines, the CTA doesn’t over-explain. it just tells the reader what to do. the product name lands last, which means the ad starts with the reader’s curiosity and ends with the brand as the place to satisfy it. that sequence is the whole engine: recognizable conflict, market-specific question, teased answer, simple subscription action.

hey, quick one — can you write an ad for Uniswap Labs? it’s for the Uniswap web app. people can use it to trade crypto tokens, it covers a really big token catalog, works across 12 chains, and it connects into Uniswap’s onchain marketplace.

ok so for this one i’d keep it super clean and direct:

Easily access thousands of tokens across 12 chains. 

The safe, simple way to swap on the largest onchain marketplace.

Uniswap Web App

the move here is that the first sentence does the whole utility job in one breath: “Easily access” makes the action feel low-friction before the reader even gets to the crypto-specific part. that matters because “thousands of tokens” could otherwise feel overwhelming; putting “Easily” first turns abundance into convenience instead of complexity.

“thousands of tokens across 12 chains” is also doing a nice scale stack. “thousands” gives breadth, then “12 chains” gives interoperability. the order is important: tokens first because that’s what a trader actually wants to reach, chains second because that’s the infrastructure proof underneath it. it reads from user outcome to technical credibility, which keeps it from sounding like a spec sheet.

the blank line after the first sentence is doing real work too. it lets the access claim land on its own, then the second line reframes the product from capability into reassurance: “The safe, simple way to swap…” that pairing of “safe” and “simple” is especially strong for this category. crypto swapping can trigger concerns around risk, complexity, and choosing the right venue, so the copy answers both anxieties with two plain words and a comma. the comma makes the phrase feel measured rather than breathless: safe first, simple second.

“way to swap” is casual and product-native without overexplaining. it doesn’t say “execute digital asset transactions” or anything heavy; it uses the verb the user already understands. then “on the largest onchain marketplace” gives authority at the end of the sentence, where it has the most weight. the line starts with reassurance and ends with dominance.

the final line, “Uniswap Web App,” is intentionally plain. no extra punctuation, no flourish. after two benefit lines, the product name acts like a clean sign-off and makes the ad feel credible. the whole thing is compact: first line = access and scale, second line = trust and ease, final line = what to use. for a brand like Uniswap Labs 🦄, that restraint helps the ad feel confident instead of salesy.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

hey, quick one for you — can you write a short social ad for Vance Ginn?

he’s got a commentary out about the killing of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson and the bigger frustration people have with American healthcare. the point is that violence is wrong, but there are real reasons people are angry about how the system works.

yep — I’d keep it tight and tension-forward:

While nothing exonerates the murder of CEO Brian Thompson, public outrage at our healthcare system is in many ways justified. 👇

Rage Against the (Healthcare) Machine

the move here is that the first sentence handles the moral tension immediately. “While nothing exonerates” is doing a lot of work: it makes the condemnation unambiguous before the ad says anything about frustration with healthcare. that matters because the topic is volatile; if the copy opened with the anger first, it could read like it was excusing the murder. instead, the first clause draws a hard ethical line, then the second clause gives the reader permission to engage with the broader critique.

“the murder of CEO Brian Thompson” is also very specific. it doesn’t soften the event with language like “incident” or “death,” and it names the person by role and name, which keeps the stakes concrete. then “public outrage at our healthcare system” widens the frame from one crime to a shared institutional frustration. the phrase “in many ways justified” is carefully calibrated too — it doesn’t say all outrage is justified, and it doesn’t overclaim. that qualifier makes the sentence feel measured rather than inflammatory.

the comma after “Thompson” is the hinge of the whole ad. everything before it is the guardrail; everything after it is the argument. that structure lets the copy be provocative without feeling reckless. it’s a compact sentence, but it has a clear moral sequence: condemn the violence, acknowledge the anger, point toward the system.

the 👇 emoji is useful because it changes the sentence from a complete opinion into a prompt to continue. it tells the reader there’s more below without needing a clunky “read more” or “learn why.” visually, it also gives the first line a social-native feel, which helps because the topic itself is heavy and policy-oriented.

then the line break creates a clean pause before the title. the ad doesn’t crowd the title into the same paragraph; it lets the argument land, then presents the piece as the thing that will unpack it.

“Rage Against the (Healthcare) Machine” works because it’s familiar without being flat. the echo of “Rage Against the Machine” gives it cultural energy, and putting “Healthcare” in parentheses makes the twist instantly visible. the parentheses are doing the wink: they signal that this is about a system, not just a single person or company. “Machine” is a strong final word because it makes healthcare feel impersonal, bureaucratic, and grinding — exactly the emotional territory the first line sets up.

overall, the ad earns attention by walking straight into the tension instead of avoiding it. it’s not long, but the order is precise: moral clarity first, shared frustration second, downward cue third, memorable title last. that’s why it can be controversial enough to stop the scroll while still feeling responsible.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.
Write ad for Premium+ on X — paid subscription tier for people using X, with a subscribe CTA.

Subscribe to Premium+ for the smoothest experience on X.

Subscribe

Lead with “Subscribe” as the first word because the ad is built as an action, not a description. The opening verb removes setup and tells the reader exactly what to do before naming the product. “Premium+” lands immediately after the verb, so the paid tier is not buried; the plus sign also visually signals the highest or upgraded option without needing extra explanation.

Keep “for the smoothest experience” as the benefit because it compresses the promise into one clean superlative. “Smoothest” is softer than a technical claim and broader than a single feature, so it can cover speed, reduced friction, cleaner use, and overall feel without listing mechanics. The phrase also sounds native to a platform subscription: the user is not buying an object, they are improving the way X feels to use.

End the sentence with “on X” to anchor the benefit in the exact environment where the reader already spends time. That final placement matters: the line moves from action, to product, to payoff, to platform. The reader gets the complete chain in one sentence: do this, get this tier, improve this experience, inside this app.

Use the period to make the claim feel settled and complete. No exclamation point keeps the tone controlled; no extra modifiers keep it from sounding overclaimed. The blank line creates a clean visual break between the promise and the action button, giving the CTA its own weight.

Repeat “Subscribe” as the button because the repetition closes the loop. The first “Subscribe” instructs; the second “Subscribe” converts. The identical wording reduces decision friction: the reader has already processed the action once, then sees the same action as the next step. Short copy, single benefit, single command, no competing idea.
